docs.title: YSE Knowledge Base
docs.text: Welcome to the internal knowledge base of Youth Security Europe (YSE). This site is for staff and volunteers and contains guides, policies, and best practices to help us work safely and effectively.

docs.title: Security
docs.text: Guide Description Phishing Awareness How to recognize and respond to phishing emails in Gmail Vishing & Social Engineering Recognizing and handling voice phishing, AI voice cloning, and manipulation attacks Incident Reporting What counts as a security incident and how to report it to the IT team

docs.title: Microsoft 365
docs.text:
Guide Description Logging In to Teams Sign in to Microsoft Teams using your YSE Google account Getting Started with Teams Messaging, calls, and scheduling meetings from Google Calendar Managing Teams and Channels How department directors manage membership, channels, and permissions Your Microsoft 365 Licence Overview of all apps included in your YSE Microsoft 365 licence

For questions or to report an issue with this knowledge base, contact the IT admin (support@youthsecurity.org).

docs.title: Your Microsoft 365 Licence
docs.text: Audience: YSE staff and volunteers with Microsoft 365 access Last updated: June 2026

docs.title: What Is Included
docs.text: Your YSE Microsoft 365 licence gives you access to a broad set of Microsoft tools beyond Teams. This page summarises what is available so you can make the most of your licence.

docs.title: Core Office Web Apps
docs.text:
These run entirely in your browser; no installation required.

App What it does Word Create and edit documents Excel Spreadsheets and data analysis PowerPoint Slide presentations OneNote Digital notebooks and note-taking Outlook Web-based email (connected to Exchange Online)

Access them at microsoft365.com and sign in with your YSE credentials (follow the same Google SSO login process as for Teams).

docs.title: Communication and Collaboration
docs.text: App What it does Microsoft Teams Chat, video meetings, calls, and file collaboration. See the Teams guide Exchange Online Business email with a 50 GB mailbox and calendar management SharePoint Online Team sites for document libraries and intranet pages OneDrive for Business 1 TB of personal cloud storage per user Microsoft Stream Video sharing and storage, integrated with Teams Viva Engage (formerly Yammer) Enterprise social networking

docs.title: Productivity and Task Management
docs.text: App What it does Microsoft Planner Kanban-style task boards for team projects Microsoft To Do Personal task lists, synced across devices Microsoft Lists Structured information tracking and smart lists Microsoft Forms Surveys, quizzes, and polls Microsoft Bookings Appointment scheduling (for external-facing bookings) Microsoft Loop Collaborative workspaces and reusable components Microsoft Shifts Staff scheduling within Teams

docs.title: Content and Learning Tools
docs.text: App What it does Microsoft Sway Digital storytelling, newsletters, and visual reports Microsoft Clipchamp Browser-based video editing Microsoft Editor AI-powered writing and grammar assistance Viva Learning Hub for training and learning content Viva Insights (Personal) Privacy-protected productivity analytics Microsoft 365 Copilot Chat AI chat with web grounding and writing assistance

docs.title: Getting Access to a Specific App
docs.text:
Most apps are available immediately once your licence is activated; just sign in at microsoft365.com.

If you cannot access a specific app or receive an "access denied" error, contact support@youthsecurity.org with the name of the app you are trying to use.

See also: Logging In to Microsoft Teams · Getting Started with Microsoft Teams

docs.title: Getting Started with Microsoft Teams
docs.text: Audience: YSE staff and volunteers with Microsoft Teams access Last updated: June 2026

docs.title: What Is Microsoft Teams?
docs.text:
Microsoft Teams is a collaboration platform for chat, video calls, and file sharing. Within YSE it is used for internal instant messaging and online meetings. Think of it as the Microsoft equivalent of Google Meet + a persistent group chat, all in one place.

New to Teams? Start here.

This guide covers the basics you need to communicate with colleagues. For step-by-step login instructions, see Logging In to Microsoft Teams.

docs.title: The Teams Interface at a Glance
docs.text:
When you open Teams you will see a sidebar on the left with the main navigation:

Icon What it does Chat One-on-one and group direct messages Teams Shared workspaces with channels (topic-based threads) Calendar Your schedule and meeting links Calls Voice and video calls Files Files shared with you across Teams and SharePoint

The search bar at the top is the fastest way to find a person, a message, or a file.

docs.title: Start a new chat
docs.text:
1. Click Chat in the left sidebar.
  2. Click the pencil/compose icon (New Chat) near the top.
  3. In the To: field, type the name or email of the person you want to reach. YSE members will appear in the suggestions as you type.
  4. Type your message in the text box at the bottom and press Enter (or click the send button) to send.

Search by name

You do not need to remember a colleague's email address. Type their first name and Teams will find them within the YSE directory.

docs.title: Reply in an existing chat
docs.text: Click on any existing conversation in the Chat list. Your new message goes in the text box at the bottom.

docs.title: Group Chats
docs.text:
To message more than one person at once:

  1. Start a new chat as above.
  2. In the To: field, add multiple names; Teams will turn the conversation into a group chat automatically.
  3. You can optionally give the group chat a name by clicking the pencil icon next to the participants at the top.

docs.title: Start a call from a chat
docs.text:
Inside any chat (one-on-one or group), use the icons in the top-right corner:

  • Camera icon → start a video call
  • Phone icon → start an audio-only call

The other participants will receive a pop-up notification to join.

docs.title: Join a scheduled meeting
docs.text: Scheduled meetings appear in your Calendar tab. Click the meeting and then Join to enter the call. See the Google Calendar section below for meetings scheduled via Google.

docs.title: Teams and Channels
docs.text:
A Team is a shared workspace for a group of people (e.g. a project team or a committee). Each Team contains one or more channels: topic-based threads where messages and files are organized.

To browse Teams you have been added to, click Teams in the left sidebar. To post a message in a channel, open the channel and use the text box at the bottom.

Note

Access to specific Teams and channels is managed by administrators. If you need to be added to a Team, contact support@youthsecurity.org.

docs.title: Recording and Transcribing Meetings
docs.text: Teams can record meetings and generate an automatic transcript. Both features are available during any call or scheduled meeting.

docs.title: Start a recording
docs.text:
1. Join a Teams meeting.
  2. In the meeting controls bar at the top (or bottom, depending on your layout), click the More menu (...).
  3. Select Start recording.

All participants are notified that the meeting is being recorded. The recording begins immediately.

Inform participants before recording

Recording a meeting without the knowledge of participants may have legal implications depending on the jurisdiction. Always inform everyone at the start of the call that you intend to record.

docs.title: Start a transcription
docs.text:
Transcription runs independently of the recording; you can have one without the other.

  1. In the meeting controls, click the More menu (...).
  2. Select Start transcription.

A live transcript panel opens on the right side of the screen, showing what each participant says attributed by name.

docs.title: Stop recording or transcription
docs.text: Open the More menu (...) again and select Stop recording or Stop transcription.

docs.title: Where to find the recording and transcript after the meeting
docs.text:
When the meeting ends, Teams processes the recording and transcript automatically. They are saved to Microsoft Stream and are accessible from the meeting chat:

  1. Open Chat in the left sidebar and find the meeting conversation.
  2. The recording appears as a playable video card in the chat, and the transcript is available as a downloadable .vtt file or viewable inline.

For scheduled meetings, you can also find them in the Calendar tab: click the past meeting entry and check the Recordings tab.

Processing time

Recordings and transcripts may take a few minutes to appear after the meeting ends, depending on the length of the call.

docs.title: Scheduling Teams Meetings from Google Calendar
docs.text: YSE Google Calendar includes a Microsoft Teams add-on that lets you create Teams meeting links directly from a Calendar event, without ever opening Teams.

docs.title: Create a Teams meeting in Google Calendar
docs.text:
1. Open Google Calendar.
  2. Click on a time slot or click + Create to open a new event.
  3. In the event editor, look for the "Add conferencing" dropdown (or a dedicated "Microsoft Teams meeting" button in the sidebar on the right).
  4. Select Microsoft Teams meeting from the options.
  5. A Teams meeting link is automatically generated and inserted into the event description.
  6. Add your guests, set the time, and click Save. Guests will see the Teams link in their calendar invite.

The Teams link works for everyone

Guests do not need a YSE Microsoft account to join a Teams meeting via a link; they can join as external guests directly from their browser.

docs.title: Join a Teams meeting from Google Calendar
docs.text:
1. Open the event in Google Calendar.
  2. Click the Microsoft Teams meeting link in the event description.
  3. Your browser will open the Teams web app (or the desktop app if installed) and place you in the meeting lobby.
  4. Click Join now once the host admits you.

Don't see the Teams option in Calendar?

The Teams add-on must be enabled for your Google Workspace account. If you do not see the option, contact support@youthsecurity.org so the admin can enable it.

docs.title: Useful Keyboard Shortcuts
docs.text: Shortcut Action Ctrl + E / Cmd + E Focus the search bar Ctrl + N / Cmd + N New chat Ctrl + Shift + M / Cmd + Shift + M Mute/unmute in a call Ctrl + Shift + O / Cmd + Shift + O Turn camera on/off in a call Ctrl + / View all keyboard shortcuts

docs.title: Need Help?
docs.text:
For login issues or account provisioning, contact support@youthsecurity.org. For general Teams questions, Microsoft's own documentation is available at support.microsoft.com.

See also: Logging In to Microsoft Teams · Your Microsoft 365 Licence

docs.title: Logging In to Microsoft Teams
docs.text: Audience: YSE staff and volunteers who have been granted Microsoft 365 access Last updated: June 2026

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before you can log in, an administrator must have granted your account access to the YSE Microsoft 365 tenant. You will receive an email notification when this is done.

If you have not received that notification but believe you need access, contact support@youthsecurity.org.

docs.title: 1. Open the Teams web app
docs.text:
Navigate to https://teams.cloud.microsoft/ in your browser.

Prefer the desktop app?

You can also download the Microsoft Teams desktop app for Windows or macOS. When prompted to sign in, follow the same steps below.

docs.title: 2. Enter your YSE email address
docs.text: Type your YSE email address (yourname@youthsecurity.org) in the sign-in field and click Next.

docs.title: 3. Sign in with Google
docs.text: Microsoft will detect that YSE uses Google as its identity provider and redirect you to the Google sign-in page. Sign in with your YSE Google account as you normally would (password, two-factor authentication if prompted).

docs.title: 4. Trust the organization (first login only)
docs.text:
On your very first login, Microsoft may display a screen asking you to grant the YSE Microsoft tenant permission to read basic profile information from your Google account.

Click "Trust organization" (or "Accept") to proceed.

Why is this prompt shown?

This is a standard OAuth consent step. It allows the YSE Microsoft 365 tenant to confirm your identity via your Google account. No additional permissions beyond your basic profile are requested.

docs.title: 5. You are in
docs.text:
You should now be signed in to Microsoft Teams and see the main interface. Use the search bar at the top to find colleagues and start a conversation.

Pilot phase

Teams access is currently being rolled out in stages. In this phase, only a limited number of YSE members have been granted access. If someone you are looking for cannot be found, they may not yet be on the platform.

docs.title: The page does not redirect to Google
docs.text: Make sure you entered your @youthsecurity.org address in step 2. If the problem persists, clear your browser cache and try again in a private/incognito window.

docs.title: I see "Account not found" or "No account with that email"
docs.text: Your account may not yet have been provisioned. Contact support@youthsecurity.org with your full name and YSE email address.

docs.title: I see "Need admin approval"
docs.text: Your account exists but has not yet been granted a Teams license. Contact support@youthsecurity.org.

docs.title: I am stuck in a redirect loop
docs.text: This can happen if you are already signed in to a personal or non-YSE Microsoft account in the same browser. Try signing in from a private/incognito window, or sign out of any personal Microsoft accounts first.

docs.title: Need Help?
docs.text:
If you encounter any issue not covered above, contact the YSE IT team at support@youthsecurity.org and include:

  • Your YSE email address
  • A brief description of what happens (and at which step)
  • A screenshot if possible

Next: Getting Started with Microsoft Teams

docs.title: Managing Teams and Channels
docs.text: Audience: Department directors and anyone responsible for managing a YSE Team Last updated: June 2026

docs.title: How YSE Uses Teams
docs.text:
Each YSE department has its own Team (a shared workspace in Microsoft Teams). Department directors are the owners of their Team and are responsible for:

  • Managing who is a member of the Team
  • Creating, editing, and archiving channels within the Team
  • Setting channel permissions

The IT admin manages the Teams tenant itself (creating and deleting Teams, assigning licenses) but does not manage the day-to-day membership or channel structure within each department's Team. That is your responsibility as a director.

Need a new Team created?

Only IT admins can create or delete a Team. If your department needs a new Team, or an existing Team needs to be deleted, contact support@youthsecurity.org.

docs.title: Understanding the Structure
docs.text: Concept What it is Team The top-level workspace for your department. Contains all members and channels. Channel A topic-based space within a Team for focused conversations and files. Standard channel Visible and accessible to all members of the Team. Private channel Visible only to specific members you invite; useful for sensitive sub-groups within the Team. Owner A Team member with management rights: can add/remove members, create/delete channels, change settings. Member A Team member with access to all standard channels. Can post and interact but cannot manage the Team.

docs.title: Add a member to your Team
docs.text:
1. In the left sidebar, click Teams and find your department's Team.
  2. Click the ... (More options) next to the Team name.
  3. Select Manage team.
  4. Click Add member.
  5. Search for the person by name or YSE email address and select them.
  6. Choose their role (Member or Owner) and click Add.

Owners can manage the Team; members cannot.

Only assign the Owner role to people who should be able to add/remove others and manage channels. For most colleagues, Member is the right choice.

docs.title: Remove a member from your Team
docs.text:
1. Go to Manage team (as above).
  2. Click the Members tab.
  3. Find the person in the list and click the ✕ to the right of their name.
  4. Confirm the removal.

Removing someone from the Team removes their access to all standard channels immediately.

Private channels have their own membership; if the person is also in a private channel, remove them there separately (see Managing Private Channels below).

docs.title: Change a member's role
docs.text:
1. Go to Manage team → Members.
  2. Click the dropdown next to the person's name (showing Member or Owner).
  3. Select the new role.

docs.title: Create a new channel
docs.text:
1. Click ... next to your Team name and select Add channel.
  2. Give the channel a clear, descriptive name.
  3. Add an optional description to help members understand its purpose.
  4. Choose the channel type:
  5. Standard: visible to all Team members
  6. Private: visible only to the members you specify
  7. Click Create.

Name channels for their purpose, not their audience.

A channel named #grant-applications is clearer than #directors-only. Good names make the workspace self-explanatory to new members.

docs.title: Edit a channel name or description
docs.text:
1. Click ... next to the channel name.
  2. Select Edit channel.
  3. Update the name or description and click Save.

docs.title: Delete a channel
docs.text:
1. Click ... next to the channel name.
  2. Select Delete channel.
  3. Confirm the deletion.

Deleting a channel permanently removes all its messages and files.

This action cannot be undone. If you want to preserve the content, consider archiving the files to SharePoint first, or simply leaving the channel in place and posting a notice that it is no longer in use.

docs.title: Reorder channels
docs.text: Click and drag any channel in the list to reorder it. The General channel is pinned at the top and cannot be moved or deleted.

docs.title: Private Channels
docs.text: Private channels are useful when a subset of your Team needs a space for sensitive discussions (for example, a directors-only channel within a broader departmental Team).

docs.title: Create a private channel
docs.text: Follow the steps under Create a new channel and select Private as the channel type. You will be prompted to add the initial members immediately.

docs.title: Manage private channel membership
docs.text:
Private channels have their own membership list, separate from the main Team.

  1. Click ... next to the private channel name.
  2. Select Manage channel.
  3. Use Add member to add people, or click ✕ to remove them.

Only members of the Team can be added to a private channel within it.

If someone is not yet in the Team, add them to the Team first (as a Member), then add them to the private channel.

docs.title: Best Practices for Directors
docs.text:
Keep membership current. When someone leaves your department or YSE, remove them from the Team promptly. This revokes their access to all files and conversations in the Team's channels.

Use channels deliberately. A Team with dozens of channels becomes hard to navigate. Start with a few well-named channels and create more only as a genuine need arises. A good default structure for most departments:

Channel Purpose General Announcements and topics that don't belong elsewhere (created automatically) #projects or topic-specific channels Active work and coordination #resources Shared documents, guides, reference material

Keep the General channel for announcements. You can configure General so that only owners can post, making it a clean broadcast channel. Go to ... → Manage channel → Permissions and set "Who can post" to Owners only.

Archive rather than delete. If a project or working group concludes, deleting its channel removes history. Instead, rename it with a prefix like [archive] and note in the description that it is no longer active, so the history stays accessible for reference.

docs.title: What Directors Cannot Do
docs.text:
Some actions require IT admin intervention. Contact support@youthsecurity.org for:

  • Creating or deleting a Team entirely
  • Changing the Team's name or external visibility settings
  • Adding someone who does not yet have a YSE Microsoft 365 account (the account must be provisioned first)
  • Recovering a deleted channel or Team

See also: Getting Started with Microsoft Teams · Logging In to Microsoft Teams

docs.title: Google Workspace Data Classification Labels
docs.text: Audience: All YSE staff and volunteers Last updated: June 2026

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Youth Security Europe (YSE) handles various types of information, from public announcements to sensitive personal data. To ensure data is handled securely, we use Google Workspace Data Classification Labels.

Information assets are classified into three categories: Public, Internal, and Confidential. Members must handle data according to the specific requirements of its classification level.

docs.title: How to Apply Labels in Google Workspace
docs.text:
(Please provide the following screenshots so they can be added to this section:) - [Screenshot 1: The label badge next to the document title in Google Docs/Sheets/Slides, or File > Labels menu] - [Screenshot 2: The label selection menu showing the Public, Internal, and Confidential options] - [Screenshot 3: Where the label is visible in the Google Drive file list or file details pane]

You can apply classification labels directly from within a Google Doc, Sheet, or Slide, or from Google Drive:

  1. Inside a Document: Click on the label badge next to the document title at the top left, or go to File > Labels.
  2. Select the Label: Choose the appropriate label (Public, Internal, or Confidential) from the menu based on the document's content.
  3. In Google Drive: You can view and manage labels in the file details pane on the right side of your Drive view.

Default Classification

Internal is the default classification for most work. If you are unsure, start with Internal.

docs.title: Level 1: Public
docs.text:
Definition: Information intended for public consumption or release. Disclosure causes no harm to the organization or individuals.

Examples: Press releases, job postings, published annual reports, marketing materials on the website.

Allowed Actions: - Sharing freely with anyone, inside or outside the organization. - Posting on social media or the public website. - Storing on personal devices without encryption.

Disallowed Actions: - Modifying content without authorization (integrity protection). - Using outdated or retracted versions for official communication.

docs.title: Level 2: Internal
docs.text:
Definition: Information strictly for internal operations. This is the default classification for most work. Unauthorized disclosure could cause confusion, reputational embarrassment, or loss of credibility.

Examples: Internal memos, unfinished drafts, handbooks, standard operating procedures, internal meeting notes.

Allowed Actions: - Sharing with any active member of the organization. - Storing on approved cloud drives (Google Workspace Shared Drives).

Disallowed Actions: - Posting publicly (social media, websites, public forums). - Sending to personal email addresses or storing on personal cloud drives (e.g., personal Dropbox/iCloud). - Forwarding to external parties without a specific operational need. Personal initiatives cannot be considered operational needs in any circumstances.

docs.title: Level 3: Confidential
docs.text:
Definition: Highly sensitive information where unauthorized access could result in significant financial loss, legal liability, physical harm, or severe reputational damage. Access is restricted strictly to "Need-to-Know."

Ownership: It is mandatory to define the owner of each document classified as Confidential; the default owner is the user who created the document.

Examples: Personally Identifiable Information (PII), donor lists, financial bank records, passwords/private keys, legal/strategy documents, private communications with external sources.

Allowed Actions: - Accessing only on devices with Full Disk Encryption enabled. - Sharing only with specific individuals who require the data to perform their job. The access for each individual must be approved by the owner of the document. - Discussing in private environments only (avoiding public spaces or open offices where screens are visible). - Deleting or shredding immediately when no longer needed.

Disallowed Actions: - Sending via standard, unencrypted email. - Downloading to local storage (hard drives, USBs) unless absolutely necessary and encrypted. - Sharing credentials via chat applications (e.g., Slack, Teams, WhatsApp). - Sharing content of such documents in cloud-based systems that do not guarantee not to store data, such as AI chatbots or assistants. - Printing on shared printers where the document might sit unattended.

Confidential Data Requires Care

Always double-check permissions when handling Confidential data to ensure only authorized individuals have access.

docs.title: Incident Reporting
docs.text: Audience: All YSE staff and volunteers Last updated: June 2026

docs.title: What Is a Security Incident?
docs.text:
A security incident is any event (or suspected event) that could compromise the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of YSE's information, accounts, or services.

You do not need to be certain that something bad has happened to report it. A strong gut feeling that something is wrong is enough. Reporting early costs nothing; reporting late can cause serious, hard-to-reverse damage.

docs.title: Examples of Incidents
docs.text: The list below is not exhaustive. When in doubt, report it.

docs.title: Account & Access Incidents
docs.text: Situation Example Unauthorized access You notice a sign-in to your Google or Microsoft account from a location or device you don't recognize Credential compromise You accidentally typed your password into a phishing page, or your password appeared in a data breach Account lockout You are unexpectedly locked out of your Google Workspace or Microsoft 365 account Suspicious account activity Emails you didn't send appearing in your Sent folder; calendar events you didn't create; files you didn't share showing as shared

docs.title: Device Incidents (Your Personal Device)
docs.text:
Because YSE does not manage devices, your personal phone, laptop, or tablet is part of YSE's security perimeter when you use it to access Google Workspace or Microsoft 365. Incidents on your device can affect the whole organization.

Situation Example Lost or stolen device A phone or laptop with an active YSE session (Gmail, Teams, OneDrive) is lost or stolen Malware or ransomware Your device starts behaving strangely after opening an attachment or visiting a website; antivirus flags a threat Unauthorized access to your device Someone else has accessed your device and may have seen or interacted with YSE accounts

docs.title: Data Incidents
docs.text: Situation Example Accidental data exposure You shared a Google Drive file or SharePoint document with the wrong person or made it publicly accessible Unintended disclosure A YSE document containing personal data (e.g., a list of members, donors, or beneficiaries) was sent to the wrong email address Data loss Important files in Google Drive or SharePoint/OneDrive have been deleted or have disappeared

docs.title: Service & Communication Incidents
docs.text:
Situation Example Phishing targeted at YSE You received a phishing email impersonating a colleague, YSE itself, or a partner, especially one that others may have received too Suspicious third-party app access You notice an unfamiliar app has been granted permission to your Google or Microsoft account Account takeover attempt You receive multi-factor authentication (MFA) prompts you did not initiate (push notifications, SMS codes): someone may be trying to log in as you

MFA prompts you didn't request are a red flag.

If you receive a Google authentication request out of the blue (a push notification, a code by SMS, or a prompt in the Authenticator app), deny it immediately and report it. Someone has your password and is trying to use it. Because Microsoft 365 access is federated through Google, a compromised Google account also means compromised access to Teams, SharePoint, and OneDrive.

docs.title: How to Report an Incident
docs.text:
Email: support@youthsecurity.org

Report as soon as possible. You do not need to investigate first or have all the answers: just tell us what you observed and when.

docs.title: What to Include in Your Report
docs.text:
The more context you can provide, the faster the team can respond. Include as many of these as you can:

  • What happened: describe what you saw, received, or noticed
  • When it happened: date and approximate time (time zones help)
  • Which account or service is affected: Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, or both
  • Which device was involved: type (phone, laptop) and operating system if known
  • What you did next: did you click a link, enter a password, deny an MFA prompt?
  • Your general location: city and country where you have been recently (e.g. "Zurich, Switzerland"). If you suspect unauthorized access, the IT admin will review your account's sign-in history; knowing your legitimate locations makes it much easier to spot access from unexpected regions.
  • Any screenshots or forwarded emails: attach them if you have them

You can report even if you're unsure.

"I think something might be wrong with my account" is a valid incident report. We would always rather investigate a false alarm than miss a real compromise.

docs.title: What Happens After You Report
docs.text:
1. Acknowledgement: you will receive a reply confirming your report has been received.
  2. Assessment: the IT admin will review the situation and determine the scope and severity.
  3. Containment: if needed, affected accounts or sessions will be secured (e.g., forced sign-out, password reset, app permission revocation).
  4. Follow-up: you will be kept informed of what was found and any actions you need to take on your end.

All reports are handled confidentially.

docs.title: Immediate Actions While You Wait
docs.text: If you believe an account has been compromised right now, do not wait for a reply; take these steps immediately and then report.

docs.title: If your Google Workspace account is affected
docs.text:
1. Go to myaccount.google.com/security.
  2. Under "Your devices", review active sessions and sign out of any you don't recognize.
  3. Change your Google password.
  4. Confirm that 2-Step Verification is enabled.

docs.title: If your Microsoft 365 account is affected
docs.text:
Your Microsoft 365 account is accessed through your Google Workspace identity; you sign in to Teams, SharePoint, and OneDrive using your YSE Google account. There is no separate Microsoft password.

This means:

  • Securing your Google account also secures your Microsoft 365 access. Follow the Google steps above first.
  • If you suspect someone has accessed Teams, SharePoint, or OneDrive without your knowledge, report it and the IT admin can review Microsoft 365 sign-in logs on your behalf.
  • You can review recent Microsoft 365 activity yourself at mysignins.microsoft.com: sign in with your YSE Google account when prompted.

docs.title: If your device is lost or stolen
docs.text:
1. Use Google's Find My Device (google.com/android/find) or Apple's Find My (icloud.com/find) to locate or remotely lock your device.
  2. Report to us immediately so we can revoke YSE account sessions on that device. This prevents anyone who has the device from accessing your Google or Microsoft data.

Do not delay reporting a lost or stolen device.

Even if the device is locked with a PIN, an active Google or Microsoft session may be accessible. The IT admin can remotely revoke those sessions, but only after you report it.

docs.title: Quick Reference
docs.text:
I noticed… Report? Immediate action Sign-in from unknown location Yes Sign out all devices via account security settings MFA prompt I didn't request Yes Deny the prompt immediately Emails I didn't send in my Sent folder Yes Change password immediately File shared with wrong person Yes Revoke sharing permissions in Drive/SharePoint Lost or stolen device with active YSE session Yes Lock device remotely; report for session revocation Unexpected app with account access Yes Revoke the app's permissions in account settings Suspicious email targeting YSE Yes Report phishing in Gmail; warn the team

To report an incident, email support@youthsecurity.org. For guidance on recognizing phishing, see the Phishing Awareness Guide.

docs.title: Phishing Awareness Guide
docs.text: Audience: All YSE staff and volunteers Last updated: June 2026

docs.title: What Is Phishing?
docs.text:
Phishing is a type of cyberattack where an attacker sends a fraudulent message, usually an email, designed to trick you into revealing sensitive information (passwords, personal data) or into clicking a malicious link or attachment.

As a security-focused NGO, YSE is an attractive target. Attackers may impersonate donors, partner organizations, government agencies, or even colleagues to gain access to our accounts, contacts, or funds.

docs.title: YSE-Specific Indicators in Gmail
docs.text: Our Google Workspace is configured with two automatic safeguards that help you immediately identify external senders. These are your first line of defense.

docs.title: 1. The [EXT] Subject Prefix
docs.text:
Any email arriving from an address outside the YSE organization is automatically tagged with [EXT] at the beginning of the subject line.

What to look for:

[EXT] Your invoice is attached: please review
[EXT] Invitation: YSE board meeting
[EXT] Urgent: action required on your account

Missing [EXT]? Be suspicious.

If someone claims to be writing from an external organization (e.g., a partner NGO, a funder, or a government body) but the subject line does not start with [EXT], the message may be spoofing an internal YSE address. Contact the apparent sender through a separate, verified channel before taking any action.

docs.title: 2. The Gmail "External" Warning Banner
docs.text:
When you open an email from outside the organization, Gmail displays a prominent "External" label or banner near the subject's name. This is a Google Workspace feature enabled by your admin.

The banner is normal, but it demands attention.

Seeing the "External" banner is expected for any email from a partner, donor, or external contact. Its job is to remind you to pause and verify before acting.

docs.title: Inspect the Sender Address
docs.text:
Display names can be forged trivially. Anyone can set their display name to "Google Support" or "YSE Finance Team." Always check the actual email address.

How to check in Gmail:

  1. Open the email.
  2. Click on the sender's display name to expand the full sender details.
  3. Look at the address in angle brackets (e.g., Google Support <support-noreply@verify-account-security.com>).

Red flag: display name ≠ email domain

Display name Actual address Verdict Google noreply@google.com Likely legitimate Google support@g00gle-alerts.com Phishing YSE Director director@youthsecurity.org Likely legitimate YSE Director youthsecurity.director@gmail.com Suspicious

Pay attention to:

  • Lookalike domains: youthsecurity.org vs youthsecuriti.org, or substituted characters (0 for o, 1 for l). Or domains we don't control: currently we only control youthsecurity.org and youthsecurity.org, if you see youthsecurity.com that's NOT us.
  • Free email providers for institutional senders: a real grant foundation will not email you from @gmail.com or @yahoo.com.
  • Subdomains used to mislead: paypal.com.verify-now.net: the actual domain here is verify-now.net, not paypal.com.

docs.title: Urgency and Pressure
docs.text:
Attackers want you to act before you think. Watch for language like:

  • "Your account will be suspended in 24 hours"
  • "Immediate action required"
  • "Confirm your identity or lose access"
  • "Wire transfer must be completed today"

Pause before you act.

Legitimate services do not demand instant action via email. If you feel rushed, that is a deliberate manipulation tactic. Slow down.

docs.title: Mismatched or Generic Greetings
docs.text:
• Generic: "Dear user", "Dear customer", "Hello". A real organization that knows you uses your name.
  • Mismatched: the email body references a service or account you do not have.

docs.title: Suspicious Links
docs.text:
Before clicking any link, hover over it (desktop) or long-press (mobile) to preview the real URL.

Ask yourself:

  • Does the domain match the organization it claims to be from?
  • Is there a long string of random characters, unusual subdomains, or a URL shortener (bit.ly, t.co)?
  • Does a "Google login" link actually point to accounts.google.com, or to something else?

Never enter your password on a page you arrived at by clicking an email link.

Go directly to the website by typing the address in the browser instead.

docs.title: Requests for Credentials or Money
docs.text:
No legitimate system will ever ask for your password via email. YSE IT will never ask for your Google password. Be extremely skeptical of:

  • Requests to "verify" or "confirm" login credentials
  • Wire transfer requests, especially if supposedly from a senior colleague
  • Gift card purchases on behalf of the organization

docs.title: Unexpected or Password-Protected Attachments
docs.text:
Be cautious of:

  • Attachments you were not expecting, even from known senders (their account may be compromised)
  • .zip, .exe, .iso, .docm, .xlsm files: these can execute code
  • PDF files asking you to "enable content" or "allow macros"
  • Password-protected archives (the password is often given in the same email; this bypasses antivirus scanning)

docs.title: Grammar and Spelling Errors
docs.text: Poor grammar, awkward phrasing, or inconsistent formatting can indicate a phishing attempt, though AI-generated phishing is increasingly polished. Do not rely on this signal alone.

docs.title: Google Workspace-Specific Phishing Tactics
docs.text: Attackers know that YSE uses Google Workspace and will try to exploit that familiarity.

docs.title: Fake Google Login Pages
docs.text: You receive an email saying your account has a security issue, and a link takes you to what looks exactly like the Google sign-in page. Check the URL bar: the real Google sign-in is always at accounts.google.com. Anything else is a fake.

docs.title: Fake "Shared Document" Notifications
docs.text:
A classic attack: you receive a notification that someone has shared a Google Doc with you. The notification looks authentic, but the link leads to a credential-harvesting page or a malicious file download.

Verify shared documents directly.

If you receive an unexpected shared-doc notification, go to drive.google.com directly (do not click the link in the email) and check if the document actually appears there.

docs.title: Google Calendar Invite Phishing
docs.text: Attackers can add calendar events directly to your Google Calendar containing malicious links in the event description. If you receive an unexpected calendar invite from an unknown person with a suspicious link, decline it and report it.

docs.title: What To Do If You Suspect Phishing
docs.text:
1. Do not click any links, open attachments, or reply to the email.
  2. Report it in Gmail:
  3. Open the email.
  4. Click the three-dot menu (⋮) in the top-right corner of the email.
  5. Select "Report phishing".
  6. Warn your team if the phishing email impersonates a colleague or is targeted at YSE specifically: others may have received it too.

docs.title: What To Do If You Already Clicked
docs.text:
Act quickly; the first few minutes matter.

  1. Do not enter any credentials if you are still on the page.
  2. Change your Google account password immediately: go to myaccount.google.com/security.
  3. Review recent sign-in activity: on the same page, check for unfamiliar devices or locations and sign out of all sessions.
  4. Check that 2-Step Verification is still enabled on your account.
  5. Notify the IT admin immediately at support@youthsecurity.org, even if you are unsure whether anything happened. Early notification dramatically limits the damage.

Do not delay reporting out of embarrassment.

Phishing attacks are sophisticated and anyone can be deceived. Reporting quickly is the single most effective thing you can do to protect the organization.

docs.title: Quick Reference Checklist
docs.text:
Use this checklist whenever an email asks you to take an action (click a link, open a file, provide information, transfer money).

Check What to look for [EXT] prefix External sender? Subject should start with [EXT] "External" banner Verify the banner appears when you open the email Sender address Expand the sender: does the domain match the organization? Urgency or pressure Legitimate requests do not demand immediate action Link destination Hover to preview: does the URL match what is claimed? Attachment type Were you expecting it? Is it a risky file type? Request type Passwords, money, or gift cards? Never via email. Verify out-of-band For sensitive requests, call or message the sender separately

If you have questions about this guide or want to report a suspicious email, contact the IT admin at support@youthsecurity.org.

docs.title: Vishing & Social Engineering
docs.text: Audience: All YSE staff and volunteers Last updated: June 2026

docs.title: What Is Social Engineering?
docs.text:
Social engineering is manipulation, not hacking. Instead of exploiting software vulnerabilities, an attacker exploits human psychology (trust, authority, urgency, fear, or helpfulness) to get you to hand over information or take an action you otherwise would not.

Phishing (via email) is the most common form, but social engineering also happens over the phone, by SMS, through fake websites, and even in person. The techniques described in this guide require no technical skill on the attacker's part; only the ability to sound convincing.

docs.title: Vishing (Voice Phishing)
docs.text: Vishing is social engineering conducted over a voice call. The attacker calls you, or tricks you into calling them, and impersonates a trusted party to extract credentials, personal information, or to get you to perform an action (e.g., approve a transfer, grant account access, share a one-time code).

docs.title: Common Vishing Scenarios
docs.text:
IT support impersonation

You receive a call from someone claiming to be from "Google support" or "Microsoft technical team." They say your account has been flagged for suspicious activity and they need to verify your identity. They ask for your password, a verification code, or ask you to grant remote access to your device.

Neither Google, Microsoft, nor YSE IT will ever call you to ask for your password or a verification code.

Legitimate support teams do not initiate calls to request credentials. If you receive such a call, hang up and contact support@youthsecurity.org to verify.

Colleague or leadership impersonation

The caller claims to be a director, coordinator, or trusted colleague, possibly someone you know by name. They ask you to urgently approve something, share a document, reset an account, or make a payment. The urgency is deliberate: they want to prevent you from thinking carefully or verifying.

External authority impersonation

The caller claims to be from a government agency, law enforcement, a bank, or a partner organization. They present a scenario that requires immediate action on your part.

docs.title: AI Voice Cloning: A New and Serious Threat
docs.text:
Modern AI tools can clone a person's voice from as little as a few seconds of audio; publicly available material such as a recorded talk, a podcast appearance, a video interview, or a social media post is more than enough.

This means an attacker can call you and make it sound exactly like your director, a trusted colleague, or a partner you know personally. The voice will be familiar. It will match the person's accent, cadence, and tone.

Hearing a familiar voice is no longer sufficient proof of identity.

If a call involves an unusual request (credentials, financial approval, access to accounts, urgent action), verify through a separate channel, regardless of how convincing the voice sounds.

How to verify out-of-band:

  • Send a message to the person's known YSE Gmail address or Teams chat and ask them to confirm the call.
  • Call them back on a number you already have stored. Do not use a number provided by the caller.
  • Ask a colleague who knows the person to verify directly.

The few minutes this takes are worth it.

docs.title: Smishing (SMS Phishing)
docs.text:
Text messages with malicious links or urgent requests. SMS is particularly dangerous because:

  • Messages feel more personal and immediate than email.
  • URLs are harder to inspect on mobile screens.
  • The [EXT] safeguards that protect your Gmail inbox do not apply to SMS.

Treat any unexpected SMS asking you to click a link, provide a code, or take urgent action with the same skepticism as a phishing email.

docs.title: Pretexting
docs.text:
The attacker constructs a believable backstory (a "pretext") before making contact. Examples:

  • Spending days exchanging friendly emails to build trust before making a request
  • Posing as a new volunteer asking innocent questions to map out YSE's structure, staff names, and processes: information that then enables more targeted attacks

Be thoughtful about how much organizational detail you share with people you cannot fully verify, even in seemingly innocent conversations.

docs.title: Quid Pro Quo
docs.text: The attacker offers something (help, information, a resource) in exchange for access or credentials. For example, someone poses as IT support and offers to "fix a problem" with your account, asking you to share your screen or provide a verification code in return.

docs.title: Tailgating and In-Person Social Engineering
docs.text:
At events, conferences, or co-working spaces, an attacker may attempt to:

  • Observe your screen or keyboard while you type credentials (shoulder surfing)
  • Pose as a delegate or partner to extract information in conversation
  • Leave USB drives or other physical media in hopes that someone picks them up and plugs them in. Never plug in a USB drive you found or received unexpectedly.

docs.title: Recognizing a Social Engineering Attempt
docs.text:
Across all channels and techniques, the same psychological levers keep appearing:

Tactic What it looks like What to do Urgency "You need to act right now or lose access" Slow down; urgency is manufactured to prevent verification Authority Impersonation of leadership, IT, government, or a known institution Verify the person's identity through a separate channel Familiarity Use of your name, colleagues' names, internal details These can all be gathered beforehand; familiarity is not proof of identity Fear "Your account has been compromised", "You will be fined" Do not act on fear; report first, act after Helpfulness Caller offers to solve a problem you weren't aware you had Unsolicited "help" requiring your credentials is almost always malicious Reciprocity Offers you something before making a request The favour creates pressure to comply; recognize the pattern

docs.title: What to Do During a Suspicious Call
docs.text:
1. Stay calm. You are not obligated to act immediately on any request, no matter how it is framed.
  2. Do not provide passwords, one-time codes, or answers to security questions over the phone.
  3. Do not grant remote access to your device to any unsolicited caller.
  4. If in doubt, end the call. A polite "Let me verify this and call you back" is always appropriate. A legitimate caller will not object.
  5. Verify independently. Use a contact method you already trust, not one provided by the caller.
  6. Report it. Even if nothing was disclosed, let us know at support@youthsecurity.org.

docs.title: What to Do If You Were Targeted or Deceived
docs.text:
1. Don't panic, and don't delay. Report to support@youthsecurity.org immediately.
  2. If you provided account credentials or a verification code, change your Google password now: myaccount.google.com/security.
  3. If you granted remote access to your device, disconnect it from the internet and contact us before using it again.
  4. Note everything you can recall: the caller's number, what was said, what information was shared, and the time of the call; include this in your report.

Do not let embarrassment delay your report.

These attacks are specifically designed to bypass the defences of careful, intelligent people. Reporting quickly gives us the best chance to contain the damage.

docs.title: Quick Reference
docs.text:
I received… Red flag Action A call asking for my password or a verification code Always Refuse, hang up, report A call from "IT support" I didn't initiate High Verify via support@youthsecurity.org before acting A call from a familiar voice requesting urgent action Possible AI voice clone Verify out-of-band before acting An SMS with a link or urgent request High Do not click; inspect carefully or report An offer to "fix" my account remotely Always Refuse; report A USB drive I didn't request Always Do not plug it in; report

To report an incident or suspicious contact, email support@youthsecurity.org. For guidance on email-based attacks, see the Phishing Awareness Guide. For general incident reporting procedures, see the Incident Reporting Guide.
